name: Oslo Badstuforening — Måken
price: Private rental: 1000 NOK/2h (members), 1300 NOK/2h (non-members); membership 100 NOK/year
highlights: Oslo's first floating sauna, hand-built by sauna enthusiasts from driftwood rescued from Bjørvika — the fjord right outside the Munch Museum. Part of a 15-sauna fleet run by the Oslo Sauna Association, with a genuinely grassroots, member-driven ethos.

name: KOK Oslo
price: Shared FellesKOK: ~320 NOK/person (1.5h); Private docked: 1280 NOK for up to 10; Cruise: 3700 NOK/2h
highlights: Wood-fired floating saunas at two central Oslo locations — beside the Opera House at Langkaia and with views of Akershus Fortress at Aker Brygge. Unique sauna-cruise option on a silent solar-electric boat; #1 Oslo spa on Tripadvisor for two consecutive years.

name: Heit Bergen Sauna — Marineholmen
address: Marineholmen, behind Bergen Science Centre (VilVite), Bergen
price: Shared: from 169 NOK (50 min) / 279 NOK (110 min); Private from 590 NOK for 2
highlights: Wood-fired floating saunas on a pier in Bergen harbour, heated with a Finnish wood-burning stove at 60–80°C. Two saunas on the same floating dock (capacity 8 and 12), with a cold plunge into Bergen's harbour between rounds. Affordable drop-in pricing makes it genuinely accessible.

name: FjordSauna Flåm
hours: Year-round, daily (summer: early morning to late evening; winter: afternoon to evening)
price: Shared: 445 NOK/person (1.5h); Private: 2990 NOK total
highlights: Boat-inspired floating sauna cabins moored on the Aurlandsfjord, surrounded by dramatic vertical walls of rock. One of Norway's most scenic sauna settings — the fjord water is cold enough year-round to make the plunge genuinely bracing. A sister sauna operates in nearby Aurland.

name: SvalBad
hours: Book online at svalbad.no; self-service with entry code sent by SMS
price: Drop-in: 300 NOK/person/hour; Private booking: 3000 NOK/2h (up to 14 people)
highlights: A wood-burning floating sauna moored in the Arctic harbour of Longyearbyen at 78°N — the world's most northerly accessible sauna. Built largely from repurposed materials salvaged from old Svalbard buildings. Views of Hiortfjellet and a real chance of northern lights from the cold plunge deck.

name: MS Vulkana
hours: Year-round; check vulkana.no for current session times
price: ~690 NOK/person/hour (seasonal variation); capacity 15 guests/hour
highlights: A 1957 Arctic fishing vessel carefully converted by architect Sami Rintala into a floating spa — with a wood-fired sauna on the main deck, a Japanese zen lounge and Turkish hammam below, and a hot tub on the viewing deck. Sails through snowy fjords in winter; a genuine piece of living maritime history.

name: Pust Sauna
price: Drop-in from 120 NOK; shared session 340 NOK/1h or 540 NOK/2h; private from 3000 NOK
highlights: Three floating sauna options — the Original (12-person electric, 80–100°C), the intimate Quiet Sauna, and the traditional Lavvo tent sauna — all with panoramic Arctic views of Tromsdalstinden and the Arctic Cathedral. One of the most accessible sauna experiences in northern Norway at a sub-Arctic harbour location.

name: Lofoten Floating Sauna — Svinøya Rorbuer
address: Svinøya Rorbuer, Lofoten (floating jetty, Svolvær harbour)
hours: Heated daily 09:00–21:00; drop-in or book in advance
price: Drop-in: 195 NOK/person/hour; private rental: 3000 NOK/2h (up to 20 people)
highlights: Wood-fired floating sauna with direct views of Svolvær harbour and the iconic twin peaks of Svolværgeita. Between rounds you leap from the floating deck into the North Atlantic — water temperature 4–14°C depending on season. Genuinely one of the most dramatic Arctic sauna settings in existence.

name: Floating Saunas at Preikestolen BaseCamp
address: Preikestolen Basecamp, Jørpeland, Ryfylke (30 min from Stavanger)
price: Approx. 400–600 NOK/person (2h session)
highlights: Wood-fired floating saunas on Refsvatnet lake in the Ryfylke wilderness, just 30 minutes from Stavanger and on the trail to iconic Pulpit Rock. The contrast of sauna heat with a dip in the cold mountain lake, surrounded by dramatic Norwegian highland scenery, is exceptional.

name: Flabellina — Floating Art Sauna
address: Mjøsvågen, Hosanger, Osterøy (45 min from Bergen)
price: Check flabellina.no for current pricing
highlights: Norway's only art sauna — a floating sculptural installation by local artists, inspired by sea slugs (Flabellina), moored in the historic fjord bay of Mjøsvågen on Osterøy. The interior and exterior are decorated as functional sculpture; wood-burning stove heats to 60–80°C with capacity for 8–10 people.

name: City Sea Sauna Bergen
hours: Tue 08:00–18:00, Wed 15:00–21:00, Sat–Sun 10:00–18:00; book at citysauna.no
price: From 150 NOK/person/hour drop-in; private cruise from 3600 NOK
highlights: Bergen's first sauna boat, with drop-in sessions and the option of a sauna cruise through Bergen's outer harbour. Modest and unpretentious — this is working-harbour sauna culture, not a luxury spa.

name: Tromsøbadet
price: ~350 NOK adult entry (includes all facilities)
highlights: Tromsø's municipal sports and wellness complex with an Olympic pool, Finnish sauna, steam bath, and an outdoor heated pool that offers potential aurora sightings on clear winter evenings. A genuine local institution — this is where Arctic residents come to swim laps and unwind after work.

name: CopenHot
hours: Daily; shared 'Hot Days' sessions at 13:00, 15:30, and 18:00
price: Check copenhot.com for current pricing; shared sessions book fast
highlights: The largest outdoor spa in Denmark, set in the post-industrial Refshaleøen shipyard district. All saunas and outdoor hot tubs are fired with wood, making the operation nearly CO2-neutral. The Dragon Sauna lets guests feed the fire themselves — old-world ritual in a new harbour setting.

name: La Banchina
price: Mon–Thu: 800 DKK/session (up to 8 people, 1h45m); Fri–Sun: 950 DKK
highlights: A 16-seat natural wine bar and organic restaurant on the Refshaleøen waterfront, with a private harbourside sauna that fits 8. The entire philosophy — no meat, local biodynamic produce, cold harbour plunge — is quietly radical. One of Copenhagen's most beloved neighbourhood spots.

name: Kastrup Søbad (The Snail)
address: Amager Strandvej, Kastrup, near Copenhagen (end of Amager Strand Park)
hours: Open year-round, 24h; changing rooms closed September–May
price: Free
highlights: An award-winning conch-shaped wooden sea bath and sauna by White Arkitekter, jutting 100m into the Øresund. Half the heating in summer comes from roof solar panels; a central wood-burning stove heats the sauna in winter. Free to enter, year-round — this is democratic Danish design at its finest.

name: Badeanstalten Spanien
hours: Fri 08:00–16:00; check visitaarhus.com for current sessions; advance booking required for wellness area
price: ~75–90 DKK for 1.5h wellness access
highlights: A Grade-I-listed 1931 functionalist public bathhouse — one of the finest examples of Nordic funkis architecture still in active use. The wellness zone includes a sauna, ice-cold plunge pool, steam bath, and spa baths. This is the genuine article: a century of Danes coming here to bathe.

name: Havnebadet Bassin 7 (Harbour Bath)
hours: Summer (Jun–Aug): daily 10:00–18:00; Winter sauna: Oct–May Sat–Sun 08:00–12:00
price: Free (harbour bath); sauna access included in season
highlights: Designed by BIG (Bjarke Ingels Group), this is the world's largest harbour bath with a 50m pool, diving pool, children's area, and two saunas, all set in Aarhus's regenerated docklands. Free entry, regular saunagus gus events with sea views — urban infrastructure that prioritises bodies in water.

name: Bølgebaderne — Kalvebod Bølge
hours: Public visitor slots: Wed 09:00–11:00 & 15:00–21:00, Sat–Sun 11:00–15:00; members only otherwise
price: 100 DKK/person/hour (visitor days); membership for year-round access
highlights: A volunteer-run winter-bathing and floating sauna club at the wave-shaped Kalvebod Bølge — part of Copenhagen's 70,000-strong winter bathing movement. The floating sauna is modest and member-led, exactly the kind of unpretentious community sauna culture that defines Danish winter bathing.

name: Helgoland — Det Kolde Gys
hours: Check club directly; public sauna hours available
price: Sauna access included with bathing club membership or guest sessions
highlights: One of Denmark's oldest winter bathing clubs, founded in 1929. The co-ed sauna designed by H+ARKITEKTER seats 50 and is built entirely from environmental materials. The famous turquoise bathing facility is a Copenhagen landmark — nearly a century of cold-water bathing culture.

name: SaunagusOdense
price: Session pricing via website; group rates available
highlights: A dedicated saunagus studio in Odense with a Finnish-style granite sauna seating 14, built with proper aspen benches and capable of reaching over 100°C. Founded by a devoted practitioner of the saunagus tradition, with 3–4 rounds of 12–15 minutes including aromatherapy and ice baths.

name: Sundhöll Reykjavíkur
price: Adults: 1,430 ISK; under 16: free
highlights: Reykjavík's oldest purpose-built indoor pool, opened 1937, designed by national architect Guðjón Samúelsson. Right in the city centre — this is where locals do their daily 'pool loop'. Dry sauna and steam bath, plus hot tubs and a 25m outdoor pool. No tourist gimmicks, just the real thing.

name: Laugardalslaug
price: Adults: 980 ISK; children 6–17: 160 ISK; under 6 & over 67: free
highlights: Reykjavík's largest outdoor pool complex: 50m lap pool, seven hot tubs (including one saltwater), sauna, water slides, and an Olympic diving area. The undisputed hub of Reykjavík's pool culture — geothermally heated, always busy with locals, and extraordinarily affordable.

name: Vesturbæjarlaug
price: Approx. 950 ISK adult
highlights: Reykjavík's most beloved neighbourhood pool, tucked in the West End near the university. Separate male/female saunas used in the nude, plus two types of sauna (dry and infrared), five hot tubs at 38–44°C, and a 6°C cold plunge. The friendliest and most un-touristy of the city's pools.

name: Seltjarnarneslaug
price: Standard municipal pool admission
highlights: The only pool in the Reykjavík capital area filled with seawater sourced from its own geothermal borehole — mineral-rich water particularly valued by people with eczema. Sauna, steam room, hot tubs, cold plunge, and a children's slide, all in the quiet town of Seltjarnarnes just 10 minutes from central Reykjavík.

name: Akureyri Swimming Pool (Sundlaug Akureyrar)
hours: Year-round; check visitakureyri.is for seasonal hours
price: Adults: ~900 ISK; under 18: free
highlights: Voted Iceland's favourite pool in 2022, this geothermally heated outdoor pool complex in North Iceland includes two 25m pools, five hot tubs (38–42°C), sauna, steam bath, cold plunge, and the longest water slide in Iceland. A 10-minute walk from Akureyri centre — completely free of tourist infrastructure.

name: Hofsós Swimming Pool (Sundlaug Hofsóss)
price: Adults: 1,000 ISK; under 16: free
highlights: An infinity-style geothermal pool on the edge of Skagafjörður fjord, with views of the island of Drangey and the surrounding mountains — widely regarded as one of Iceland's most scenic pools. Designed by the same architect as the Blue Lagoon. The pool serves a village of only a few hundred people, and tourists share it with neighbours.

name: Mývatn Nature Baths (Earth Lagoon)
address: Jarðbaðshólar, 660 Mývatn (2km east of Reykjahlíð on Ring Road)
hours: Year-round; check earthlagoon.is or myvatnnaturebaths.is for seasonal hours
price: ~3,500–5,000 ISK adult (prices vary by season)
highlights: Geothermal baths and sauna heated purely by a volcanic fissure — steam rises directly through the floor. The mineral-rich, silica-blue water (36–40°C) is drawn from up to 2,500m depth. A dry sauna and steam room complement the outdoor baths. Far from Reykjavík on the Ring Road, with almost exclusively local and Ring Road visitors.
